ops.title: wire.Server concurrent-pool admission + shutdown-drain invariant
ops.body:
**Root cause of the macOS-only CI hang (fixed 2026-07-18).** `Server.dispatch` (classConcurrent) originally did a non-blocking `select { case s.queue <- j: default: reject }` against a `Backlog`-sized queue. With `Backlog:0` the send succeeds only if a worker is *instantaneously* blocked on `range s.queue`; on a slow/loaded scheduler (macOS) the worker isn't ready yet, so the send fails and the request is spuriously REJECTED. `TestConcurrentPoolRejectionProvesNonDispatch` then blocks forever on `<-started` (the rejected request's handler never runs to close it) → the `wire` package "never prints" and the job hangs to the 10-min cap. Linux scheduling never triggered it.

**Fix, two parts:**
1. A `slots chan struct{}` of capacity `Workers+Backlog` is the admission counter, acquired non-blocking in dispatch (`default → reject`) and released by the worker after `invoke`. This decouples *capacity admission* from *worker readiness*.
2. After acquiring a slot, the enqueue is an **unconditional blocking send** `s.queue <- j` — NOT a `select` with a `<-ctx.Done()` escape.

**Why the unconditional send is correct and cannot deadlock (the load-bearing invariant):** slots caps in-flight at `Workers+Backlog`; running ≤ Workers; so buffered-or-enqueuing = in_flight − running ≤ Backlog, and the queue buffers exactly Backlog. An admitted request (slot held) therefore always has buffer space or an about-to-dequeue worker — the send blocks at most transiently, never forever.

**Why the `<-ctx.Done()` escape was WRONG (it caused an intermittent regression):** `shutdown()` runs `cancelServe()` **then** `connWG.Wait()` then `close(queue)`. cancelServe cancels every request ctx *before* draining. An admitted request caught mid-enqueue would take the ctx.Done() branch and be DROPPED — `TestShutdownSettlesQueuedWork` saw `settled=3, want 4` intermittently (1/8 local runs). Admitted work must always drain to a terminal response; client-disconnect/shutdown cancellation is observed by the worker inside `invoke`, never by dropping pre-enqueue. `connWG.Wait()` before `close(queue)` guarantees the send never races the close (no send-on-closed panic).
